nodes.label: Linear Algebra
nodes.description: This is the study of linear maps. These abstract objects form the natural language of quantum mechanics so it is essential for all aspiring physicists to learn this

nodes.label: Calculus
nodes.description: Calculus is all about how things with change with time so it is a crucial tool.

nodes.label: Classical Mechanics
nodes.description: You may have studied mechanics in school using the idea of forces. It turns out there are different ways to formulate mechanics using Lagranians and Hamiltonians

nodes.label: Quantum Mechanics for Finite Systems
nodes.description: Quantum mechanics applied to systems with discrete, finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces

nodes.label: Quantum Mechanics for Continuous Systems
nodes.description: Quantum mechanics for systems with continuous degrees of freedom

nodes.label: Group Theory
nodes.description: The mathematical study of symmetry through groups

nodes.label: Differential Geometry
nodes.description: The study of geometry using calculus and linear algebra

nodes.label: Classical Field Theory
nodes.description: Classical physics described by fields (e.g., electromagnetism, fluid dynamics)

nodes.label: Special Relativity
nodes.description: Einstein's theory of space-time for inertial frames

nodes.label: Classical Gauge Theory
nodes.description: Classical field theories with gauge symmetries

nodes.label: Quantum Field Theory
nodes.description: Quantum mechanics combined with special relativity and field theory

nodes.label: Complex Analysis
nodes.description: The study of functions of complex variables

nodes.label: Lie Theory
nodes.description: The study of continuous symmetry through Lie groups and Lie algebras

nodes.label: General Relativity
nodes.description: Einstein's theory of gravitation describing spacetime curvature

edges.description: Linear algebra provides the mathematical framework for finite-dimensional quantum systems
Linear algebra provides essential tools for differential geometry
Differential geometry is essential for understanding general relativity
Classical field theory extends classical mechanics to continuous systems
Classical gauge theory generalizes classical field theory with gauge symmetries
Quantum mechanics for continuous systems extends finite-dimensional quantum mechanics
Classical mechanics can be quantized to yield quantum mechanics for continuous systems
Calculus tools are used extensively in classical mechanics
Group theory describes the symmetry groups underlying special relativity
General relativity extends special relativity to include gravity and curved spacetime
Group theory provides the foundation for Lie theory
Differential geometry provides the geometric structure for Lie groups and manifolds
Lie theory describes the gauge symmetries used in classical gauge theory
Classical gauge theory can be quantized to yield quantum field theory
Calculus concepts support core techniques in complex analysis
Complex analysis methods appear throughout quantum field theory
Calculus provides core tools used throughout differential geometry
Quantum mechanics for continuous systems extends to quantum field theory
